title: Marta Complex

jokes.text: Anniversary? You asked me to throw a Valentine's Day party. — Did I? — You tricked me. — I deceived you, Mom. Tricked makes it sound like we have a playful relationship.
jokes.setup: Lucille accuses Michael of tricking her into throwing an anniversary party instead of a Valentine's Day party.
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects her: 'Tricked makes it sound like we have a playful relationship' — he admits to deception while also brutally characterizing their cold, adversarial mother-son dynamic.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional because it does three things at once: Michael admits wrongdoing, defines the nature of his relationship with Lucille, and delivers a razor-sharp character joke — all in one short line. Could only come from this specific relationship. The word substitution ('deceived' vs. 'tricked') is the joke, and the implication lands with devastating efficiency. Among the best jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Touché. Lupe, untie the balloons!
jokes.setup: Michael has just bested Lucille in a verbal exchange with a well-placed distinction.
jokes.punchline: Lucille concedes with 'Touché' — then immediately pivots to barking an order at her housekeeper, treating the party changeover as purely transactional and re-establishing her control.
jokes.explanation: The 'Touché' is a tiny concession that immediately transitions to imperious command. Perfect Lucille characterization — she acknowledges defeat only to reassert dominance 0.5 seconds later. Economy is near perfect.

jokes.text: There was somebody for a while, but it was too much of a brother... bother.
jokes.setup: Michael is asked if there's anyone he's interested in, and nearly says his feelings for Marta were 'too much of a brother' — catching himself because Gob is his brother.
jokes.punchline: He covers with '...bother,' but the slip is visible and the narrator has already told us the truth. The near-Freudian slip 'brother/bother' is the wordplay.
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in layered wordplay. The Freudian slip 'brother/bother' perfectly encapsulates the episode's central conflict — Michael's feelings for his brother's girlfriend. The line works on its own as a pun, but also reveals character and plot. The narrator's setup makes the audience aware of exactly what Michael almost said. Extremely quotable, huge rewatch value. Justifies the 8.5 — comparable to the calibration examples in density of meaning per word.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'That can't feel good.' — after Michael says 'It's fine. I'm over it.'
jokes.setup: Michael claims he's totally fine being alone and is over his feelings for Marta.
jokes.punchline: The narrator simply editorializes: 'That can't feel good' — a dry, knowing aside that punctures Michael's performance of okayness.
jokes.explanation: The narrator as comedian is one of Arrested Development's signature moves. Four words doing maximum work — sympathy, irony, and character commentary in one beat. Clean and effective.

jokes.text: Well, if you call not filing for divorce 'something right.'
jokes.setup: Michael has just said Lindsay and Tobias must be 'doing something right' after 14 years together.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's bar for marital success is simply not having formally dissolved the marriage. Dark and perfectly in-character.
jokes.explanation: Economical character joke. The faint praise of 'not filing for divorce' as a marital achievement is perfectly calibrated to Lindsay's nihilism. Good character integration — the sentiment is extremely Lindsay.

jokes.text: Carl Weathers' 'Baby, you got a stew goin'' — first instance, about leftover chicken bones.
jokes.setup: Tobias is paying Carl Weathers as a drama coach and asks about being a respected vs. beloved actor.
jokes.punchline: Carl deflects any acting discussion to lecture on frugal cooking — taking leftover chicken bones, throwing them in a pot with broth and a potato: 'Baby, you got a stew goin'.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout recurring jokes. The gag is that Carl Weathers — a legitimate celebrity — is such a cheapskate that he turns every conversation into a tutorial on making free food from scraps. 'Baby, you got a stew goin'' is immediately iconic. The specificity (leftover bones, broth, a potato) and the complete non-sequitur from Tobias's actual question make it land hard. Justifies 8+ — it's genuinely novel, perfectly structured as a running gag that will pay off again, and highly quotable.

jokes.text: Carl Weathers describing the Showtime movie 'Hot Ice' with Anne Archer, never touching his per diem, using craft service: 'Baby, I had a stew goin'.' — second instance.
jokes.setup: Carl Weathers says he wants to tell a 'little story about acting' — implying actual professional wisdom is coming.
jokes.punchline: The story is entirely about how he scavenged free food on a movie set, capping with the same catchphrase. The escalation: now the stew has a celebrity anecdote backstory.
jokes.explanation: The callback/escalation of the stew joke is even funnier than the original because it adds a celebrity name-drop to the cheapness and repeats the punchline verbatim. The repetition is the joke — Carl Weathers is not just frugal, he is constitutionally incapable of not making it about the stew. High craft for a running gag.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I think I'd like my money back.' — after two consecutive stew lectures instead of acting advice.
jokes.setup: Carl Weathers has given zero acting advice and delivered two elaborate stew-making tutorials.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's defeated, completely reasonable request for a refund is the most logical response possible — and therefore funny in context.
jokes.explanation: A solid reaction beat that caps the stew sequence. Tobias's resigned practicality plays against type slightly (usually he's oblivious) which makes it land. Good economy.

jokes.text: George Michael prays for a 'sign' about his crush on Maeby — looks at a candy heart that reads 'Maybe Tonight.'
jokes.setup: George Michael, agonized about his feelings for his cousin Maeby, literally asks the universe for a sign.
jokes.punchline: He gets a candy heart that says 'Maybe Tonight' — which he reads as cosmic permission. The narrator then notes it's a very popular slogan and 'the chances were one in eight.'
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed two-part joke. The setup (desperate kid asking for a sign) is relatable. The punchline (sign says 'Maybe Tonight') is perfectly absurd given the taboo context. The narrator's methodical deflation — 'one in eight' — is the killer second punch. The precision of '1 in 8' is funnier than 'very common' would be. Dark, absurdist, and character-driven. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Actually, "Maybe Tonight" was a very popular candy slogan, and the chances were one in eight.'
jokes.setup: George Michael interprets the candy heart as a miraculous personal sign.
jokes.punchline: The narrator provides clinical statistical context that completely deflates the mystical reading.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'one in eight' is the joke — it's not just 'common,' it's precisely, bureaucratically ordinary. The narrator's deadpan statistical precision is a signature AD move that works brilliantly here. Near-perfect economy.

jokes.text: George Michael throwing candy at Tobias's head while 'just tryin' to throw stuff at my dad's head, but the wind keeps taking it.'
jokes.setup: Tobias asks what George Michael is doing on the balcony.
jokes.punchline: He's casually, matter-of-factly trying to hit Tobias in the head with candy — his only complaint is wind interference.
jokes.explanation: The deadpan delivery of a mildly aggressive act (throwing things at dad's head) as a totally normal activity is funny. The complaint about the wind is the kicker — the practical obstacle to petty vengeance. Good character work for George Michael's simmering frustration.

jokes.text: George Michael gives Maeby the 'Maybe Tonight' candy heart, calling it a 'one-in-a-million thing' that 'made me think of you.'
jokes.setup: George Michael has just been told by the narrator (and the audience knows) the heart is 1-in-8 common.
jokes.punchline: He presents it to Maeby as a miraculous, unique token of affection — the audience's superior knowledge makes this cringe-funny.
jokes.explanation: Strong use of dramatic irony. The audience laughs at George Michael's sincerity precisely because we've just been told the scientific truth. Works well as a callback that pays off immediately.

jokes.text: Lucille invites Carl Weathers to dinner on the one night Tobias has class, then 'innocently' suggests Carl and Tobias have dinner alone.
jokes.setup: Tobias tells his mother he has class on Tuesday — the night Carl Weathers is invited.
jokes.punchline: Lucille says 'Oh, how silly of me' — then suggests Carl and Tobias have dinner alone, transparently engineering a situation to drive a wedge into Tobias's relationship (so he'll be available for her).
jokes.explanation: Lucille's transparent scheming delivered with faux-innocence is a reliable character joke. The 'Oh, how silly of me' is a nice Lucille beat. Character-specific and functional.

jokes.text: Carl Weathers: 'I never say no to dinner, with a beautiful lady — that is. Do you like ham?' 'No. I love it.'
jokes.setup: Carl Weathers is invited to dinner by Lucille. He gives a gallant compliment, then asks a mundane question.
jokes.punchline: Lucille answers 'No. I love it' — correcting his understatement of her enthusiasm for ham with characteristic dramatic flair.
jokes.explanation: 'No. I love it' is a small but perfectly Lucille line — she doesn't merely like things, she loves them intensely. The escalation from 'like' to 'love' for ham is an absurd but characterful beat.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'This was supposed to be my anniversary toast.' / Michael: 'And this was supposed to be my Valentine's Day party.'
jokes.setup: The infomercial has replaced what was supposed to be a personal anniversary message.
jokes.punchline: Michael's deadpan callback to the party-vs-anniversary confusion from the opening scene mirrors Lindsay's complaint exactly.
jokes.explanation: Clean callback that rewards attentive viewers. Michael and Lindsay's parallel disappointments are structurally satisfying.

jokes.text: The family chants 'Speech, speech, speech' for no one in particular — Narrator confirms: 'The family continued to chant for no one in particular.'
jokes.setup: After the infomercial plays and everyone is vaguely disappointed, someone starts the 'Speech' chant.
jokes.punchline: There is literally no designated speaker. The chant builds and continues for an absurdly long time with no target, confirmed by the narrator's clinical observation.
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of the Bluth family mechanically chanting 'speech' with no designated target is a good ensemble joke. The narrator's confirmation ('for no one in particular') adds the dry kicker that makes it land.

jokes.text: 'After all that, I was kinda hoping somebody would make a speech.'
jokes.setup: The entire family has chanted 'speech' at length but nobody has actually given one.
jokes.punchline: Michael voices the obvious absurdity with understatement — he was, in fact, hoping for a speech after all that.
jokes.explanation: Functional beat that caps the chant absurdity. Michael as straight man voicing reasonable exasperation is well-executed but not remarkable.

jokes.text: Buster says 'Don't forget my girlfriend' to Michael's toast — Michael says 'That's who I meant' — implying he just confused Buster's girlfriend Lucille 2 with his own mother Lucille.
jokes.setup: Michael toasts 'to Buster and Lucille' meaning his mother.
jokes.punchline: Buster thinks Michael is toasting his girlfriend Lucille 2 and enthusiastically includes her; Michael's quick 'that's who I meant' covers the confusion — revealing both that Buster is dating someone with his mother's name and that Michael forgot to account for Lucille 2.
jokes.explanation: A subtle but well-layered joke for AD fans — the two Lucilles are a recurring source of confusion and the show leans into the Oedipal implications. Michael's smooth cover is funny because it's not a cover at all.

jokes.text: Michael nearly says 'George Michael and Maeby' in his toast, then catches himself — 'I almost said George Michael and Maeby.'
jokes.setup: Michael is giving a sweet family toast, going through the couples.
jokes.punchline: He nearly pairs George Michael with Maeby — inadvertently acknowledging the taboo crush that the audience knows about but Michael doesn't fully know about — then laughs it off.
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony joke — Michael's near-slip has more resonance than he realizes. The audience cringes because they know the full truth. Works well in context.

jokes.text: Michael concludes his toast 'To love and happiness' — immediately cuts to Marta saying 'I love you all, Marta' and realizing it was Michael she wanted.
jokes.setup: Michael's toast seems to be a selfless, family-centered speech about love.
jokes.punchline: His toast accidentally functions as a confession of feeling to Marta, who has been listening and interprets it as directed at her — 'I made a huge mistake' (re: being with Gob).
jokes.explanation: The show's 'I've made a huge mistake' catchphrase gets a new speaker (Marta) in a romantic context, which is a satisfying evolution of the running gag. Well-structured scene-level irony.

jokes.text: Lindsay asks Michael for divorce advice, referencing his toast — 'Should I use Barry or just bolt in the night? I'm leaning toward bolt in the night.'
jokes.setup: Michael gave a heartfelt toast about honesty and healing — Lindsay took it as permission to divorce Tobias.
jokes.punchline: Her preferred method is 'bolt in the night' — a hilariously undignified and cowardly exit strategy that she's already clearly thought through.
jokes.explanation: 'Bolt in the night' as a serious, carefully-considered legal strategy is perfect Lindsay — impulsive, dramatically avoidant, and somehow both cowardly and decisive. The 'I'm leaning toward' implies deliberation, making the absurdity funnier.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'In fact, Tobias was a never-nude. As of yet, the D.S.M.-IV has not acknowledged the affliction.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay describes Tobias as 'psychologically incapable of taking all of his clothes off.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator names the condition ('never-nude'), treats it as a legitimate psychological affliction, and notes with faux-regret that the DSM-IV hasn't caught up yet.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the great Arrested Development jokes. 'Never-nude' as an official-sounding diagnosis, paired with the clinical note that the DSM-IV 'has not as yet acknowledged the affliction,' treats an absurd character quirk with complete bureaucratic seriousness. The joke is layered: (1) the condition is ridiculous, (2) the narrator's gravity is ridiculous, (3) the specificity of citing an actual psychiatric manual elevates it to parody of medicalization culture. Quotable, perfectly constructed, and pure Tobias. Justifies 9+ — this is the caliber of the 'snip snap' reference example.

jokes.text: Michael says he's not judging — 'On the other hand, I am doing it anyway, so let me suggest...'
jokes.setup: Michael starts to take the moral high ground on Lindsay's marriage situation.
jokes.punchline: He immediately self-corrects: he IS judging, and he's fine with that. The meta-self-awareness of admitting to judging while claiming not to is perfectly Michael.
jokes.explanation: A sharp character moment — Michael's self-righteous self-awareness is a recurring trait. The pivot 'on the other hand, I am doing it anyway' is economically funny.

jokes.text: 'I was gonna take her with me in the night. She's part of the bolt.'
jokes.setup: Michael points out Lindsay has a kid and can't just walk out.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay has already planned for this — Maeby is included in the 'bolt in the night' scheme. She's logistically considered the bolt.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the 'bolt in the night' joke. Lindsay's matter-of-fact inclusion of her daughter as 'part of the bolt' is funny for how casually she treats her child as luggage in an escape plan.

jokes.text: Buster tells Michael he loved the speech — 'I never want to look at Mom again.'
jokes.setup: Michael is glad his toast about family and being true to oneself 'made an impact.'
jokes.punchline: Buster's takeaway from Michael's speech about love and family is to cut off his mother completely. A wildly disproportionate reaction.
jokes.explanation: The gap between Michael's gentle speech and Buster's nuclear interpretation is a great character beat — Buster's Oedipal intensity means any message about independence becomes a declaration of war against Lucille. Well-character-integrated.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Do you know she tried to set up my girlfriend with a man?'
jokes.setup: Buster is outraged at Lucille's scheming.
jokes.punchline: His framing of Lucille setting up his girlfriend 'with a man' as an egregious betrayal is funny because (a) it's exactly what happened and (b) Buster says it with full horror, oblivious to the Oedipal dynamic at play.
jokes.explanation: Buster's outrage at a perfectly normal social act (setting someone up on a date) is funny because of his pathological dependence on his mother-figure girlfriend. Solid character comedy.

jokes.text: Well, I can't really talk. She might be listening in.
jokes.setup: Buster calls Michael to talk about his mother
jokes.punchline: Despite calling Michael specifically to talk, Buster immediately says he can't talk because his mother might be listening — revealing the total irrational control she has over him
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the contradiction: he called to talk, but he can't talk. The paranoia about Lucille listening reveals the depth of his psychological dependence. Character-perfect Buster.

jokes.text: Lucille interrupts the call: 'Can I throw out this yogurt?' — Buster responds with two simultaneous conversations, Michael has to manage both.
jokes.setup: Buster is on the phone with Michael having a serious conversation about moving out and independence from Lucille.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's oblivious yogurt question interrupts the call, Michael has to navigate giving advice to both simultaneously — the mundanity of 'can I throw out this yogurt' punctures the emotional urgency.
jokes.explanation: Good situational comedy — the yogurt question as interruption to a serious independence talk is a nice tonal contrast. The subsequent three-way phone confusion is funny but not perfectly crisp.

jokes.text: Michael advises Buster to move out — adds 'you don't want to take any chances with yogurt, Mom. Just let her throw it out' — addressing both simultaneously.
jokes.setup: Michael is managing a phone call where both Buster and Lucille need advice simultaneously.
jokes.punchline: Michael seamlessly code-switches between life-changing advice ('I think it might be time for you to move out') and yogurt safety guidance, delivering both with equal gravity.
jokes.explanation: The yogurt gets equal billing with Buster's existential crisis. Michael's total pragmatic neutrality across both problems is funny.

jokes.text: Three-way phone call confusion: Michael says 'nobody' is with him (Marta is there), Gob says 'well now I don't know what's going on.'
jokes.setup: Gob tells Michael: 'If Marta's there, say nobody.' Michael says 'nobody.' But Marta IS there.
jokes.punchline: The code word system immediately breaks because Michael uses it incorrectly, and Gob's exasperated 'well now I don't know what's going on' acknowledges the total failure of the scheme.
jokes.explanation: Classic farce structure — the code word plan collapses instantly. Gob's helpless confusion after his own system fails is the right reaction and well-timed.

jokes.text: Gob says Marta 'wouldn't let me make love to her on Mom's bed' and 'I don't even want to tell you what she wouldn't let me do to her in the car.'
jokes.setup: Gob is listing evidence that Marta is cheating on him.
jokes.punchline: His evidence is that she refused sex on his mother's bed and in a car. That he considers these reasonable requests — and their refusal suspicious — characterizes Gob perfectly.
jokes.explanation: 'Wouldn't let me make love to her on Mom's bed' — Gob's baseline for normal relationship behavior is perfectly revolting and specific. The ominous 'I don't even want to tell you what she wouldn't let me do in the car' is well-judged restraint. Strong Gob characterization.

jokes.text: Marta on the phone says 'Hermano' — Gob thinks it's the name of a man she's cheating with. Narrator reveals she was talking to her mother about Michael.
jokes.setup: Gob overhears Marta saying 'Hermano' repeatedly on the phone and concludes she's talking about a man named Hermano.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals she was talking to her mother about Michael — 'Hermano' means 'brother' in Spanish, and she was referring to Michael as Gob's brother. Gob's jealousy is entirely based on not knowing Spanish.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's central comic engine. The 'Hermano' misunderstanding is elegant: it drives the entire A-plot, has a satisfying revelation, plays on Gob's comical ignorance, and has a perfect payoff when Michael interrogates the soap opera cast. The comedic irony runs deep — Gob is chasing a phantom rival who is literally his own brother. Justifies 8.7 in craft.

jokes.text: 'If anyone's gonna go out with that girl, it's gonna be one of us. Right. Me. And I'm okay with that.'
jokes.setup: Gob tells Michael they're going to track down 'Hermano' together and 'nail him.'
jokes.punchline: Gob's motivation for helping find the rival is explicitly to claim Marta himself. Michael agrees ('right, me') but Gob accepts this with 'and I'm okay with that' — completely missing that Michael also wants her.
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered: Gob reveals selfish motives, Michael reveals his feelings, and Gob's obtuse 'I'm okay with that' shows he doesn't register Michael as a rival. The whole Bluth dynamic in four lines.

jokes.text: Tobias says cousins of opposite gender shouldn't share rooms — George Michael argues 'bunkin' cousins' is a term. Tobias: 'Kissin' cousins.' George Michael: 'We're not kissing! That's the point!'
jokes.setup: Tobias tells George Michael he can't share a room with Maeby anymore. George Michael argues there's nothing wrong with cousins bunking together.
jokes.punchline: George Michael invents 'bunkin' cousins' as a phrase; Tobias corrects it to 'kissin' cousins,' which George Michael panics about and immediately disavows — his over-denial reveals exactly what he's trying to hide.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cringe comedy. George Michael's invented word 'bunkin' cousins' is funny on its own. Tobias's correction to 'kissin' cousins' and George Michael's explosion — 'We're not kissing! That's the point!' — is a perfect example of protesting too much. His denial reveals the precise thing he's denying. Justifies 8.5 — perfectly structured, pure character integration, and deeply uncomfortable in the best way.

jokes.text: Cousins can bunk together. That's why they call it 'bunkin' cousins.' 'Kissin' cousins.' We're not kissing! That's the point!
jokes.setup: Tobias says cousins of opposite gender shouldn't share a room; George Michael argues they should
jokes.punchline: George Michael's logic: 'bunkin' cousins' — his invented phrase, immediately corrected to 'kissin' cousins' by Tobias, which George Michael panics to deny, revealing how much is on his mind
jokes.explanation: A layered wordplay/cringe moment. George Michael invents 'bunkin' cousins' to justify something innocent; Tobias corrects it to 'kissin' cousins' — the actual phrase — which George Michael then desperately denies. The denial reveals his guilt. The joke works because the slip from 'bunkin'' to 'kissin'' traverses exactly the territory George Michael is trying to suppress. Excellent structure and character integration.

jokes.text: 'I'm gonna sleep with my daughter, and you're gonna sleep with my husband.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is explaining the new sleeping arrangements after Tobias moves in with George Michael.
jokes.punchline: The sentence Lindsay says is technically accurate but sounds scandalous out of context — Lindsay will share a room with Maeby, Tobias will share with George Michael — but the phrasing is maximally wrong.
jokes.explanation: A good economy joke — the line is structurally perfect because it sounds horrible but is innocent. Lindsay's total obliviousness to how this sounds is in character.

jokes.text: Buster reading from a note: 'Because you know I don't appreciate how you treat me. I am not a child, Mother.' — then: 'Blow.' / 'No, Mother! I can blow myself.'
jokes.setup: Buster is reading a prepared speech to Lucille about his independence — a big emotional moment where he asserts his manhood.
jokes.punchline: Lucille holds a tissue to his nose and says 'Blow.' Buster reflexively starts to comply, then catches himself and indignantly says 'No, Mother! I can blow myself.' — which sounds obscene and also immediately undermines his entire independence speech.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's funniest moments. The double meaning of 'I can blow myself' is accidental from Buster's perspective but devastating from the audience's. More importantly, Lucille's tissue-nose maneuver during his declaration of independence is a perfect visual embodiment of their entire dynamic — she treats him as a child at his most assertive. This is deep character comedy that also happens to be wildly funny as wordplay. Justifies 8.6.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'So, Buster loaded up his possessions and emancipated himself from his mother.' — Visual of Buster presumably carrying a tiny amount of belongings dramatically.
jokes.setup: Buster has dramatically declared his independence from Lucille.
jokes.punchline: The narrator describes his departure with legal/formal language ('emancipated himself') as though a grown man moving out is a momentous legal and personal act. Visually Buster's 'possessions' are presumably minimal — the gravitas applied to the mundane act is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The word 'emancipated' applied to a middle-aged man moving out of his mother's house captures the infantilization perfectly. Narrator-as-comedian doing good work.

jokes.text: Buster arrives at Lucille 2's apartment. Carl Weathers is there. 'This is purely a social call. Just two adults gettin' a stew on, man.'
jokes.setup: Buster is outraged to find Carl Weathers at Lucille 2's apartment.
jokes.punchline: Carl Weathers describes the situation as 'gettin' a stew on' — a callback to his culinary catchphrase but now deployed as a euphemism for socializing (possibly romantically). The phrase's new context makes it both a callback and a plausible innuendo.
jokes.explanation: The stew idiom evolves from frugal cooking tip to possible social/romantic euphemism. Buster's disgusted 'I don't know what that means but it sounds disgusting' is the perfect reaction — he half-understands it might be a euphemism but can't parse it. Good escalation of the running gag.

jokes.text: Oh, hey, Buster. I thought you had class. I thought you had class. My brother-in-law fires you... And you move in on my girlfriend?
jokes.setup: Buster arrives at Lucille 2's apartment to find Carl Weathers there
jokes.punchline: Buster accuses Carl Weathers of 'moving in on his girlfriend' — Carl's casual reply 'I thought you had class' mirrors what Buster just said to him, escalating the confrontation absurdly
jokes.explanation: The mirrored 'I thought you had class' exchange is a nice structural echo. The absurdity of Buster accusing Carl Weathers — who has already been established as extremely charming and socially smooth — of a romantic incursion is characteristically Bluth-family-paranoid.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I don't know what that means, but it sounds disgusting.'
jokes.setup: Carl Weathers says they were 'gettin' a stew on.'
jokes.punchline: Buster accurately identifies that the phrase sounds wrong without being able to articulate why.
jokes.explanation: Buster's half-comprehension is the joke — he's both right and unable to explain why. The line is quotable and perfectly timed as a reaction beat.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I think the world is about to fall in love with Frightened Inmate #2.'
jokes.setup: Carl Weathers got Tobias an audition for a role.
jokes.punchline: The role is 'Frightened Inmate #2' — the most anonymous, undignified bit part imaginable — but Tobias announces it with full theatrical grandeur.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tobias joke. 'Frightened Inmate #2' is the funniest possible role name — not even #1, and paired with an emotion that undercuts dignity. Tobias's unironic pride in this role is the entire character in miniature. Highly quotable. Justifies 8.5.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Carl Weathers took everything from me. My entire life just came crashing down... I will never love again.'
jokes.setup: Buster arrives at the model home in crisis, having just found Carl Weathers at Lucille 2's apartment.
jokes.punchline: Buster's hyperbolic tragedy — 'I will never love again' — over a social visit is grotesquely overblown and perfectly Buster. The escalation to cosmic despair is instant.
jokes.explanation: Buster's emotional spiral is in-character and funny, but 'I will never love again' over jealousy about a social call is a slightly predictable beat for his character at this point.

jokes.text: Buster sees Marta and immediately thinks: 'I've been dating an old lady.'
jokes.setup: Buster arrives devastated, then Marta — beautiful, young — walks in.
jokes.punchline: Buster's entire worldview shifts in an instant: compared to Marta, his girlfriend is 'an old lady.' The narrator reveals his inner monologue with deadpan simplicity.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully efficient. 'I've been dating an old lady' — four words that (1) insult Lucille 2, (2) reveal Buster's Oedipal pattern is now visible even to him, (3) set up a new plot complication, (4) undercut his earlier vow of never loving again with immediate infatuation. The economy here is exceptional. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: You're replacing my mother. [Lucille 2 to Buster] / You need some time. [long pause, door closes]
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 tells Buster he's replacing his mother with her
jokes.punchline: Buster's 'Huh?' — complete incomprehension — followed by the door closing on him; the joke is that the diagnosis is entirely correct and he cannot even begin to process it
jokes.explanation: The awkward silence after 'Huh?' — the door closing on Buster's confusion — is a perfectly executed physical/silence joke. Buster's inability to process accurate psychological insight about himself is both funny and sad. Classic AD tonal blend.

jokes.text: Michael asks a soap opera crew member 'I'm looking for Hermano' — everyone points to 'their brother.' He follows the chain of pointing across the entire set.
jokes.setup: Michael asks crew members where 'Hermano' is — each one says 'mi hermano?' and points to their brother.
jokes.punchline: The pointing chain leads Michael around the set as everyone interprets the question as asking about their own brother. He follows the chain to its absurd conclusion.
jokes.explanation: This is the Hermano gag at its most brilliantly structured. The chain of pointing is a perfect physical-comedy payoff: each crew member legitimately has a brother ('mi hermano?') named by Michael's question. It works as pure misdirection and as an escalating physical gag. The visual of Michael being passed from person to person across a soap opera set is inherently funny. Justifies 8.5.

jokes.text: 'Son of a bitch.' — Michael's reaction when the chain of pointing leads back to the beginning or a dead end.
jokes.setup: Michael has followed a chain of 'hermano' pointers across an entire soap opera set.
jokes.punchline: His quietly furious 'son of a bitch' lands as the punctuation mark on the absurd sequence — both funny as profanity from the show's straight man and as a recognition that the joke is on him.
jokes.explanation: The straight man's expletive as punchline to a physical comedy sequence. Clean and well-placed. Michael cursing is mildly surprising given his usual control.

jokes.text: Lucille, alone in her apartment, says she's 'never lived in the apartment alone' and fears she's 'not up to this.'
jokes.setup: Buster has just left. Lucille tells Michael she's scared to be alone.
jokes.punchline: Lucille — the most controlling, imperious woman on the show — is paralyzed by basic solitude. The inversion of her usual omnipotence is funny.
jokes.explanation: Competent character reversal joke — Lucille is exposed as dependent. Functional but not particularly fresh for this character.

jokes.text: Michael cites Caged Wisdom to Lucille — 'There is joy in solitude, Lucille. Try being alone. It just might make you happier.' Lucille: 'Honey, my happiest times here are alone.' — Immediately asks the building manager about the 'hole being available between 4:00 and 6:00.'
jokes.setup: Michael encourages Lucille to embrace solitude using George Sr.'s prison self-help tape.
jokes.punchline: Lucille says her happiest times are alone — then immediately calls for the building's amenity (implied to be a gentleman caller or some intimate arrangement) for a 4-6pm slot, making clear what 'alone time' means to her.
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered: Lucille agrees she loves being alone while immediately arranging to not be alone. The question about the 'hole' is deliberately ambiguous and Michael's 'I don't even want to know what that means' is the right response. Good dark Lucille characterization.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I don't even want to know what that means.'
jokes.setup: Lucille asks Bruno about 'the hole being available between 4:00 and 6:00.'
jokes.punchline: Michael draws a firm line at curiosity — the best reaction to Lucille's apparent arrangement is determined ignorance.
jokes.explanation: Clean straight-man reaction. Economy near perfect — four words that define Michael's relationship with his mother's inner life.

jokes.text: She's at the house. She's at the house! I'm gonna speak very carefully in case she's with you. You're right. Right about what? She's there! What's going on?
jokes.setup: Michael is with Marta when Gob calls; Gob tells him to speak carefully
jokes.punchline: Gob's paranoid instruction ('speak very carefully') leads to Michael saying 'you're right' — which means nothing, and Gob interprets it as confirmation she's there, creating a spiral of miscommunication
jokes.explanation: A solid escalating miscommunication joke. The coded phone call conceit — speak carefully, say the opposite, then lose track of what's code and what isn't — is a classic comedy structure executed competently.

jokes.text: Narrator describes Tobias's audition: 'The competition frightened Tobias, which he felt he could use in his performance. Unfortunately this made him more confident, which frightened him again.'
jokes.setup: Tobias arrives at the audition for 'Frightened Inmate #2' and sees the competition.
jokes.punchline: The narrator describes a recursive loop: fear makes him confident, confidence makes him afraid, which is useful. It's an absurd psychological pretzel that perfectly captures Tobias's catastrophically over-analytical approach to acting.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist character comedy. The recursive fear-confidence-fear loop is logically coherent within Tobias's world but externally insane. The narrator delivering this as dry explanation is perfect. The structure is airtight: setup the loop, then land 'which frightened him again' as the punchline. Among the episode's best jokes — justifies 8.7.

jokes.text: Lindsay to Maeby: 'Having him in the next room makes me miss him all the more.' — immediately undercut by: 'Mom, please. He's got a big-boy crush on Action Jackson.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay claims she and Tobias have a 'wonderful, close relationship' despite their separation.
jokes.punchline: Maeby matter-of-factly names the actual issue: Tobias has a crush on Carl Weathers (nicknamed 'Action Jackson'). Lindsay immediately pivots: 'I know. How can I compete with that?'
jokes.explanation: 'He's got a big-boy crush on Action Jackson' is a great line — the nickname 'Action Jackson' for Carl Weathers is perfect, and Maeby's bluntness in naming the obvious dynamic (that even a teenager can see) while Lindsay was performing ignorance is sharp. Lindsay's defeated 'how can I compete with that?' is wonderfully self-pitying.

jokes.text: 'No! We may have to bolt.' — Lindsay tells Maeby not to unpack her suitcase because the divorce plan is still on.
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just told Maeby everything is fine and they're not going anywhere.
jokes.punchline: Two seconds later, when Maeby starts to unpack: 'No! We may have to bolt.' — the 'bolt' plan is back, instantly contradicting her reassurance.
jokes.explanation: Good callback and character consistency — Lindsay's reassurance lasts about 10 seconds. 'We may have to bolt' as a standing emergency protocol is funny.

jokes.text: Tobias to George Michael: 'Do you know the last time I made love to my wife?' / George Michael: 'No.' / Tobias: 'I'll tell you when.' / George Michael: 'No, don't.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is sharing deep marital grievances with his teenage nephew.
jokes.punchline: The rhythm of 'I'll tell you when' / 'No, don't' is perfectly timed — George Michael's refusal is the correct and extremely relatable response to being invited into adult TMI territory.
jokes.explanation: The comedic rhythm here is near-perfect. Three exchanges, perfectly timed. The setup ('I'll tell you when') implies he's about to share, George Michael's instant 'No, don't' is the audience's surrogate response. The structure mimics a classic comedy exchange with no wasted syllables. Justifies 8.3.

jokes.text: Tobias reveals the only reason Lindsay married him was 'to piss off her mother' — and they couldn't conceive — setting up George Michael's realization that Maeby might not be his cousin.
jokes.setup: Tobias is listing the fundamental flaws of his marriage in a cascade of revelations.
jokes.punchline: The cascade lands on 'we couldn't conceive' — and George Michael suddenly realizes Maeby might be adopted, meaning she might not be his cousin. His face (inferred): 'Is she just some girl I know?'
jokes.explanation: This is an excellent escalation joke that also functions as a major plot development. Each revelation (married to piss off her mother, couldn't conceive) would be funny on its own, but the final beat — George Michael's dawning realization 'Is she just some girl I know?' — takes a dark family secret and reframes it through his taboo-crush perspective. The joke and the plot are perfectly fused. Justifies 8.6.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Yes, I'm the doctor. The perfect husband. The big manly man. The big strong daddy.' — full self-pity list to George Michael.
jokes.setup: Tobias is lamenting his marriage to his teenage nephew.
jokes.punchline: The list of identities he rattles off — 'the big manly man, the big strong daddy' — is undercut by every visible fact about Tobias, who is none of these things. Delivered in ironic self-mockery but lands as unintentional comedy because Tobias genuinely believes these are his roles.
jokes.explanation: Tobias listing hypermasculine roles he clearly does not inhabit is a reliable character joke. 'The big strong daddy' especially — given everything we know about Tobias — is funny. The inappropriateness of sharing this with George Michael adds discomfort.

jokes.text: Tobias gets the call that he booked 'Frightened Inmate #2' — 'Did you hear that, Lindsay? You could have been sleeping with Frightened Inmate #2.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is wallowing in marital failure when his phone rings.
jokes.punchline: He yells the triumphant news to Lindsay through the wall — framing the achievement of 'Frightened Inmate #2' as something she should be jealous of missing out on.
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the 'Frightened Inmate #2' setup is executed perfectly. Tobias's pride in the role and his framing of it as a lost opportunity for Lindsay ('you could have been sleeping with...') is simultaneously self-aggrandizing and pathetic. The callback lands hard. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Lucille, alone, turns on TV — George Sr.'s Caged Wisdom is playing. She turns it up to maximum volume and realizes she likes being alone.
jokes.setup: Lucille was terrified of being alone. She watches TV for company.
jokes.punchline: The Caged Wisdom tape — intended to teach her to enjoy solitude — works, but only because she discovers she can turn the volume up as loud as she wants with no one to disturb. Her joy in solitude is discovered through the petty pleasure of unrestrained TV volume.
jokes.explanation: A satisfying character callback — George Sr.'s self-help tape works, but through the most Lucille-specific mechanism possible (loud TV). The joke is that the lesson is right but the motivation is petty. Narrator's dry delivery of the realization is perfect.

jokes.text: Michael and Marta have a dinner conversation about relationships that is simultaneously about completely different things — he thinks she's talking about Gob; she's talking about Michael himself.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to suss out Marta's feelings about Gob. Marta is trying to tell Michael she has feelings for him.
jokes.punchline: Every line they exchange applies to both conversations simultaneously — dramatic irony at its most sustained. 'Finding out who you really are can be painful.' 'You can't live a lie.' Both think they're communicating; neither is.
jokes.explanation: Sustained dramatic irony scene. Works well on rewatch when you know both sides. The scene is a little slow in the episode but the comic structure is sound — two people talking past each other perfectly. Scored modestly because the comedy is more clever than laugh-out-loud funny.

jokes.text: What the hell is he talking about? [Michael's confessional cut to Marta] What is she talking about? [Marta's cut]
jokes.setup: Michael and Marta have been having the most non-communicative dinner conversation possible
jokes.punchline: Cutting to each of them separately asking the exact same question about the other reveals they are equally lost
jokes.explanation: The parallel cut structure — each character asking the exact same question about the other — is a clean, economical visual joke that perfectly caps the dinner scene. The structural symmetry is the joke: their mutual incomprehension is perfectly matched.

jokes.text: Michael bribing Marta's young son with cake to get information about 'Hermano.' 'I'll give you what you want if you give me what I want. Tell me everything you know about... Hermano.'
jokes.setup: Michael can't get information from Marta so he turns to her child
jokes.punchline: The adult corruption of a small child with cake to extract intelligence is played as a suave noir move — 'tell me everything you know about... Hermano' delivered dramatically to a kid
jokes.explanation: The image of Michael doing a theatrical noir interrogation of a small child using cake as leverage is absurdly funny. The delivery of 'Hermano' with dramatic gravity to a kid makes the absurdity of the entire investigation clear.

jokes.text: Michael bribes Marta's young son with cake to get information about 'Hermano' — 'I'll give you what you want if you give me what I want. Tell me everything you know about Hermano.'
jokes.setup: Michael can't get information from Marta so he turns to her young child.
jokes.punchline: Michael is essentially bribing a small child with cake to spy on his mother — and phrases it as a hardboiled detective deal. The gap between Michael's self-image as moral center and what he's actually doing (interrogating a toddler) is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy — Michael's desperation to find 'Hermano' has led him to interrogate a child over cake. The noir-ish framing of the exchange ('if you give me what I want') applied to a kindergartner is funny.

jokes.text: Does your mother love 'Hermano?' Sí, mi hermano. He's here? Sí. He's sleeping. Don't go in there.
jokes.setup: Michael asks Marta's son about 'Hermano' — the boy answers 'sí, mi hermano' (yes, my brother)
jokes.punchline: Michael thinks 'Hermano' is hiding in the bedroom — he's actually about to discover Marta's literal sleeping brother, not a lover
jokes.explanation: The final escalation of the 'Hermano' joke before full revelation. Michael thinks he's found the lover's lair; it's a brother's bedroom. 'He's sleeping' adds perfect absurdist detail — the 'rival' is literally napping. Well-structured payoff beat.

jokes.text: Michael confronts Marta about 'lying and sneaking around, moving on to the next guy' — not realizing she's trying to tell him SHE'S moving on to HIM.
jokes.setup: Michael, believing Marta is cheating on Gob, begins a moralistic confrontation about honesty.
jokes.punchline: Every accusation he makes is simultaneously a description of his own situation — he's lecturing her for what she's doing to Gob while doing the same thing (pursuing his brother's girlfriend). Complete dramatic irony.
jokes.explanation: Strong dramatic irony. Michael's self-righteous speech about honesty applies perfectly to his own secret feelings — the irony is not subtle but it's well-earned. His lecture about 'lying and sneaking around' while conducting a secret investigation of his brother's girlfriend is the comic heart of the scene.

jokes.text: We can keep going around in circles, but maybe one of us should just come out and say what it is we want to say. / You're right. / It's not working with Gob.
jokes.setup: Michael challenges Marta to be direct; she agrees
jokes.punchline: Marta's direct statement is about Gob — not a confession of love for Michael, which is what he (and we) expected. Michael's subsequent lecture about 'sneaking around and moving on to the next guy' is completely wrong.
jokes.explanation: A good misdirection: Michael's expectation (and the audience's) that honesty will lead to romantic revelation is subverted when Marta simply says it's not working with Gob — which Michael then catastrophically misinterprets. The setup for Michael's self-righteous speech in the wrong direction is well-constructed.

jokes.text: Marta: 'I should have told him before I invited you here. This can never be.'
jokes.setup: Michael has just accused Marta of cheating and sneaking around. Marta agrees he's right.
jokes.punchline: Her agreement and 'this can never be' sounds like a confession to Michael's accusations — but she's actually agreeing that she should have broken up with Gob before inviting Michael over, because she has feelings FOR Michael. The lines are perfectly ambiguous.
jokes.explanation: Effective misdirection — the lines work in both contexts. 'This can never be' is both a romantic regret and a confirmation of his accusations. Well-structured ambiguity.

jokes.text: This can never be. [Michael declares, storming out after completely misreading the situation]
jokes.setup: Michael has delivered a self-righteous speech about honesty and values — based entirely on a misreading of the situation
jokes.punchline: 'This can never be' — delivered with tragic finality, when actually everything is fine and Marta was confessing feelings for him
jokes.explanation: Michael's theatrically noble exit — 'This can never be' — based on a completely wrong interpretation is a perfect irony payoff. The episode's central comedic engine (characters talking past each other) reaches its climax here. The economy of 'This can never be' as a tragic exit line from someone who's wrong about everything is excellent.

jokes.text: Michael sees the gift bag, asks: 'What does te amo mean?' / 'I love you.' / '...And who's in there?' / 'Mi hermano.' / 'Brother.' / 'I've made a huge mistake.'
jokes.setup: Michael sees the gift Marta bought and finds a note in Spanish — 'te amo.' He asks what it means. She says 'I love you.' He asks who it's for — 'mi hermano.' The gift is for her brother.
jokes.punchline: The entire 'Hermano' investigation collapses in one exchange. 'Te amo' = 'I love you' (to her brother). 'Mi hermano' = the actual 'Hermano' = her brother. Michael, who thought he was uncovering an affair, has instead been pursuing a family gift. 'I've made a huge mistake' — another use of the running gag.
jokes.explanation: The episode's comedic apex. Everything about this moment is perfectly engineered: (1) The Hermano misunderstanding resolves with maximum humiliation for Michael, (2) 'Te amo' momentarily sounds like evidence of the affair before being explained as familial love, (3) 'I've made a huge mistake' completes the running gag but now belongs to Michael — the one character who never uses it — making it feel earned, fresh, and devastating. The structure across the entire episode builds to this 8-second payoff. Economy is extraordinary — the reveal happens in about five words. This is the caliber of television comedy that rewards rewatching and belongs in discussions of the show's best moments. Justifies 9.3.

jokes.text: Gob's phone call: 'Maybe because you've only had sex four times.' / 'Four women! Not four times! And they talk to me because I talk to them.'
jokes.setup: Gob says women feel safe around Michael — implying he's unthreatening.
jokes.punchline: Gob's explanation is that Michael has 'only had sex four times' — meaning he thinks Michael is sexually inexperienced and therefore non-threatening. Michael's furious correction ('four women, not four times') accidentally sounds even worse.
jokes.explanation: Classic setup/punchline with a hidden second punch. Gob's insult ('only had sex four times') is bad; Michael's correction ('four WOMEN') makes it sound worse. The joke works on two levels: Gob's casual cruelty and Michael's inability to defend himself without deepening the wound. Highly quotable and pure Gob-Michael dynamic. Justifies 8.2.

jokes.text: Preview: Tobias has a shower scene for Frightened Inmate #2 — 'There's a shower scene? I have to be nude.' / 'Well, you don't shower with your clothes on, now, do ya?'
jokes.setup: Tobias booked the role of Frightened Inmate #2. The preview reveals there's a shower scene.
jokes.punchline: For a never-nude, being required to be actually nude for a shower scene is the perfect comic punishment. The director's 'you don't shower with your clothes on' is hilariously obvious — and for Tobias, existentially threatening.
jokes.explanation: Perfect convergence of two running gags: the never-nude condition and the Frightened Inmate #2 role. The shower scene is the logical, inevitable, most perfectly cruel outcome for a never-nude actor. The director's baffled common sense ('you don't shower with your clothes on') against Tobias's existential panic is expertly constructed. Justifies 8.8 — this is elite AD callback engineering.

jokes.text: Buster rebounds immediately from heartbreak over Lucille 2 — 'Yo te quiero mucho. Te amo, Marta. Te amo.' — in the 'On the next Arrested Development' preview.
jokes.setup: Buster swore he'd 'never love again' after the Lucille 2 situation. Minutes later he saw Marta and had a revelation.
jokes.punchline: The preview shows Buster now declaring love to Marta in Spanish — ironically using the same phrase ('te amo') that just resolved the Hermano plot — and doing it badly.
jokes.explanation: The 'On the next' preview uses the episode's Spanish vocabulary callback to set up the next conflict. Buster pivoting from 'I will never love again' to professing love in broken Spanish is fast and funny. The callback to 'te amo' having just been used in the Hermano reveal adds a layer.

tmdb_overview: When Lucille throws an anniversary party for Tobias and Lindsay on Valentine's Day, Michael gives an impassioned speech about love. This makes Marta realize she has feelings for him and Lindsay realize she wants a divorce. Meanwhile, Buster decides to move out of the penthouse and Lucille revels in living alone for the first time.
insight: Michael's romantic delusion escalates across 62 jokes at 2.45-per-minute peak density.
